topics.title: Distributed Systems — CAP Theorem, Sharding & Replication
topics.description: Distributed systems spread data and computation across multiple machines. CAP theorem states you can guarantee only 2 of 3: Consistency, Availability, Partition Tolerance. Since network partitions always occur in distributed systems, the real choice is CP vs AP. Replication distributes data copies for read scaling and fault tolerance. Sharding (horizontal partitioning) distributes data for write scaling and storage. Understanding these tradeoffs is mandatory for system design interviews.
topics.story: Imagine a distributed bank with branches in Mumbai and Delhi. When the network between them fails (partition), you have a choice: refuse to serve customers (CP — consistent but unavailable) or serve them with possibly outdated balances (AP — available but inconsistent). There is no option C that is both perfectly consistent and always available during a partition. Real systems (DynamoDB = AP, HBase = CP, most RDBMS = CP) make this choice based on their use case.
topics.analogy: CAP: Consistency = all nodes return same data at same time. Availability = every request gets a response. Partition Tolerance = system works despite network splits. CP = bank ATM during network outage: refuses transaction (consistent, unavailable). AP = DNS: returns possibly stale cached data (available, eventually consistent). Replication: primary-replica (MySQL read replicas) or multi-primary (Cassandra). Sharding: user IDs 1-1M → shard A, 1M-2M → shard B (range sharding). Hash sharding: shard = hash(userId) % N.
topics.memoryAnchor: CAP: pick 2. Network partitions always happen → real choice is CP vs AP. PACELC extends CAP: P→(C vs A), E(lse)→(L[atency] vs C[onsistency]). Replication lag: replica may be seconds behind primary. Strong consistency: all reads see latest write. Eventual consistency: reads will converge to latest write eventually. Read your own writes: a user sees their own updates (session consistency). Shard key: low cardinality = hot spot. Consistent hashing: minimize resharding when nodes join/leave. Virtual nodes: each physical node owns multiple virtual positions in the ring.
topics.firstPrinciples: In a distributed system, you cannot distinguish a slow node from a dead node before a timeout — this is the essence of the partition problem. CAP theorem (Brewer, 2000, proved formally by Gilbert & Lynch, 2002): in the presence of a network partition, a distributed system can either respond with consistent data or respond at all (but not both). BASE (Basically Available, Soft state, Eventually consistent) is the AP alternative to ACID. Two-phase commit (2PC) achieves distributed consistency but blocks if coordinator fails.
topics.bruteForce: Single server: no sharding, no replication — single point of failure, vertical scaling limit. Master-slave with synchronous replication: writes block until all replicas confirm — high consistency but low write throughput. Manual sharding: application picks shard — adding a shard requires rebalancing all data.
topics.optimizedApproach: Read replicas for read scaling, asynchronous replication for write throughput. Consistent hashing for shard assignment — adding a node migrates only N/total_nodes data. Write to primary, read from replicas with acceptable lag. CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation): separate write and read models. For strong consistency: use distributed transactions sparingly (Saga pattern or 2PC with careful design).
topics.whenToUse: Replication: when read traffic exceeds single-server capacity (add read replicas). Sharding: when write traffic or data volume exceeds single server (horizontal scale). Eventual consistency: user feeds, like counts, view counts — minor inconsistency acceptable. Strong consistency: financial transactions, inventory management — consistency critical. Avoid distributed transactions when possible — use Saga/outbox pattern instead.

examples.title: CAP Theorem in Practice — CP vs AP Design Choices
examples.explanation: AP systems allow divergence during partitions and converge on healing — this is 'eventual consistency'. The choice of conflict resolution strategy matters: last-write-wins (timestamp), max-wins (counters), vector clocks (causal ordering), or application-level merge. CP systems sacrifice availability to maintain consistency: Zookeeper requires quorum (majority of nodes) to respond — if you can't reach a majority, you refuse the request.
examples.realWorldUse: DevLearner: user XP and streak — CP (MySQL with ACID transactions, consistency critical). Leaderboard view counts — AP acceptable (Redis counter, eventual sync to DB). System design interview: when asked 'what happens if a node fails?', answer in terms of CP vs AP: 'We chose AP here because a stale view count is better than an error for the user.'

examples.title: Consistent Hashing — Sharding with Minimal Resharding
examples.explanation: Consistent hashing places both servers and keys on a virtual ring. Each key is assigned to the first server clockwise from it. When a server is added or removed, only keys between the new server and its predecessor need to move — approximately N/total_nodes fraction. Virtual nodes distribute the load evenly when physical nodes have different ring positions. TreeMap.ceilingEntry() efficiently finds the next clockwise server.
examples.realWorldUse: Redis Cluster uses consistent hashing with 16384 hash slots. Amazon DynamoDB uses consistent hashing internally. Memcached clients use consistent hashing for cache key distribution. Cassandra uses a variant (random partitioner or Murmur3 partitioner) with virtual nodes. CDN edge routing: route user to nearest PoP using consistent hashing.

problems.title: Simple Hash vs Consistent Hash Resharding
problems.description:
Implement the Saga pattern for distributed transactions.

**Order Saga (Choreography-based):**
```
OrderService → publishes OrderCreated
PaymentService listens → charges card → publishes PaymentCompleted (or PaymentFailed)
InventoryService listens → reserves items → publishes InventoryReserved (or InventoryFailed)
ShippingService listens → schedules delivery → publishes ShippingScheduled

On PaymentFailed:
PaymentService → publishes PaymentFailed
OrderService listens → cancels order
```

**Compensating transactions:**
- Each step has a compensating action (refund, release inventory, cancel shipment)
- Executed in reverse on failure

**Key insight:** Saga replaces 2-phase commit (which requires distributed locks). Each service handles its own transaction + emits events. Eventual consistency, not ACID. Use idempotency keys to handle duplicate events.
problems.hint1: With N=3 shards, key K goes to K%3. With N=4, key K goes to K%4. How many keys change?
problems.hint2: For keys 0-11 (12 keys): calculate both K%3 and K%4. Count mismatches.
problems.hint3: Consistent hashing: only keys on the arc between old and new node positions move (~1/N fraction).
problems.testCases:
Moved: 9/12 = 75%
Consistent hashing: only ~25% would move
problems.sampleOutput: Compensating txns run: inventory released, order cancelled

problems.title: Replication Lag Impact
problems.hint1: This violates 'read-your-own-writes' consistency (also called session consistency).
problems.hint2: Strategy 1: read from primary after writes. Strategy 2: sticky session — route user to same replica.
problems.hint3: Primary reads reduce replica benefit. Sticky sessions complicate load balancing.
problems.sampleOutput: DB query; user cached for 30min; subsequent calls hit cache

problems.title: Design a Sharding Strategy
problems.pattern: Sharding design, shard key selection, trade-offs
problems.hint1: Shard key should distribute writes evenly and colocate data for common queries.
problems.hint2: user_id as shard key: all posts for a user are on one shard (efficient user feed query).
problems.hint3: Cross-user queries (trending posts, global feed) require scatter-gather across all shards.
problems.sampleOutput: Exactly 1 node wins (Redis SET NX is atomic); others get false
